narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pathways, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your application. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from past students to predict the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated an 82.3% chance that you might not pass, which is why your application was rejected—it's a safeguard to help universities select those most likely to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school: your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the 2nd decile compared to the typical 5th or 6th for most students, had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, suggesting it signaled potential struggles ahead on the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile ranking in the 2nd decile—again lower than average—reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, while below the average of 3.21, actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, showing some resilience in your college performance. Your LSAT score of 31, lower than the typical 36.7, contributed modestly toward the failure prediction, as did your upper-middle family income bracket, which didn't provide as much of a boost as it might for others. Being a full-time student worked a little in your favor, aligning with the vast majority who pursue law this way. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your lower early academic deciles stand out as the key gaps, while your background as a Black woman highlights the importance of targeted support to overcome systemic hurdles others don't face. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many with similar starts have succeeded with extra preparation. To move forward, we recommend focusing on boosting your academic deciles through targeted tutoring or retaking key exams like the LSAT, and seeking mentorship programs designed for underrepresented students like yourself to build a stronger profile for your next application. You're capable of turning this around, and we're rooting for your success in law. Keep pushing forward.
